title: Crew Archetypes
description: The specialists you'd find on a score in an industrial-gothic city. Roll for the role and the twist that makes this particular practitioner distinctive.

entries.result: The Whisper. A spirit-medium who reads ghosts the way other thieves read locks. Hears the dead constantly and has learned to nod politely; the trouble is some of them have started nodding back.
The Slide. A confidence artist who can sit at any table in any tavern and walk out with a name, a key, or a debt. Pays for it with a face that won't stay the same in mirrors after dark.
The Leech. A back-alley surgeon and chemist whose tinctures keep the crew on their feet. Each cure costs the patient something they won't notice missing until they need it.
The Hound. A tracker who can follow a man through a snowstorm by the smell of his fear. Has a registered patron-spirit and the city ministry has the papers on it.
The Hammer. Muscle, plain and practical, with a temperament colder than the work demands. Carries a saint's medal in the off-pocket and won't say which saint or why.
The Locksmith. Cracks safes, wards, and confessions with the same patient hands. Speaks to mechanisms like they're old colleagues and most of the time they answer.
The Shroud. The crew's killer, quiet and accurate, who has never been seen working. Believes the people they kill follow them home and has rented a second flat for the company.
The Ledger. Negotiator, contract-reader, and translator of patron-language. Can spot a binding clause hidden in three layers of grammar; cannot, despite trying, get out of their own.
The Smoke. Burglar and rooftop runner who treats the city's chimney-tops as a road network. Has been declared dead twice by the parish registry and finds the paperwork useful.
The Wick. Alchemist and demolitionist whose hands smell like brimstone and lemon-oil. Was apprenticed to the Ministry and walked out the night before the loyalty oath.
The Fog. Counterfeiter, forger, identity-broker. Owns a print shop above a butcher's and the inks they mix won't dry properly under direct sunlight.
The Veil. A binder of small spirits, the kind that fit inside watches, lockets, and the back rooms of pubs. Trades in obligations; the crew should never accept a gift from them on a feast day.
The Echo. A duelist and pistolier who learned their footwork from a saber-ghost that still corrects their stance. The ghost was a better fencer in life and resents it.

The Tongue. Linguist, translator, and reader of the dead languages the city was built in. Cannot speak above a whisper since reading something they shouldn't have, and writes everything down on cigarette papers.

The Splice. A clockwork-mechanic and saboteur who builds the crew's pneumatic gear, lockpicks, and breaching charges. Their left hand is a prosthetic of their own design and it occasionally writes things they did not.
The Hollow. A driver — coach, tram, motorboat, whatever the score needs — with reflexes too good for human nerves. Made an arrangement with something during the war and pays the interest in dreams.
The Mark. A face-thief and infiltrator who can pass for nearly anyone after an hour's observation. Has lost track of which face is original and uses a portrait above the mantel as a reference.
The Rind. A salvager who works the canal grates and pulls out things the city would prefer to forget. Knows the silt of every district by taste and won't go near the Old Quarter docks.
The Stitch. Healer, bone-setter, and unlicensed surgeon who patches the crew between scores. Their workshop has a back room that hums faintly and a door that's never been opened in the present tense.
The Knock. A summoner of small useful things — finder-spirits, distraction-imps, signal-wisps. Pays them in tobacco and soft songs and is nearly always slightly late because of it.
The Pin. A pickpocket and cutpurse whose fingers are quicker than reasonable. Was taught the trade by a hand-spirit they keep in a velvet glove and consult on difficult lifts.

The Gable. A cat-burglar who specializes in upper stories and rooftops, having an arrangement with the city's pigeon-spirits for guidance. The pigeons are increasingly demanding and recently asked for tribute.
The Page. A document-runner and archive-thief who can copy a ministry file by candlelight in the time it takes to drink a cup of tea. Has never learned to read out loud and won't say why.
The Switch. A poison-cook and apothecary whose potions are precise to the grain. Lost their sense of smell after a job went sideways and now identifies compounds by touch and color, never by error.
The Brand. A spirit-marker who paints binding sigils on doors, windows, and skin. The marks are flawless; the painter wakes some mornings with sigils on their own back they don't remember drawing.

The Sentry. Lookout and signaler who can hold a rooftop watch through a winter night without flinching. Has not slept more than two hours at a stretch since the war and refuses to discuss what they saw there.

The Sway. A counter-talker who can argue a constable out of an arrest, a magistrate out of a writ, a creditor out of a collection. Pays a debt of words to a tongue-spirit and is occasionally rendered mute mid-sentence.

The Brick. A demolition specialist and structural reader who can collapse a building exactly where they want it. Treats every wall as a contract between stone and gravity and apologizes before each charge.

The Cuff. A grappler and brawler trained in one of the old harbor-styles, fighting tight to the body. Wears their patron's mark as a tattoo across the knuckles and the mark warms before any real fight.
The Lacquer. A cosmetic-artist and disguise specialist whose work survives close inspection. Their kit includes a paint-pot that doesn't reflect in mirrors and they don't remember acquiring it.

The Bell. A signaler and lookout who works the canal-system, communicating across the city through bell-codes and lantern-flashes. Hears bells the rest of the crew doesn't and won't say where they're ringing from.

The Flask. A drinker, social lubricant, and information-sponge whose tolerance is unnatural. Doesn't get drunk because the patron-spirit takes the intoxication as payment, leaving them sharp and slightly less themselves each night.
The Tally. A counter, a bookkeeper, and a cooler head than any score deserves. Keeps the crew's ledgers in a script no one else can read and refuses to teach it because the script reads back.

The Scarf. A pickpocket-of-the-mind, a pulled-thread interrogator, who can take a memory out of someone's head if they get close enough. The memories pile up in a locked drawer at home and have started rearranging themselves.
The Lash. A whip-and-grapple specialist for canal-side and warehouse work. The whip was once an eel-thing from the deep harbor; it remembers being alive and occasionally acts on its own initiative.

The Coat. The crew's quartermaster, fixer, and supplier — knows every fence, every back-door tailor, every blackmarket warehouse. Owes seventeen separate debts across the city and keeps a precise mental ledger.

The Wisp. A flame-conjuror and lantern-handler whose fires are blue and don't consume their fuel. Was a gas-lighter for the city before the trade went bad; the gas-spirits remember and follow them around.

The Plumb. A surveyor, mapper, and infiltration planner whose memory for layouts is total. Hand-draws blueprints from a single walk-through; the drawings sometimes include rooms that exist but shouldn't.
The Husk. A gravedigger turned crew member who knows which corpses talk and which don't. Most cemeteries in the city won't admit them past the gates anymore; this is mutual.

The Latch. A lockpick and door-charmer who works exclusively with persuasion and patience, never force. Doors open for them, not always because of the picks. Strangers' homes too, sometimes, when they're walking past.

The Salt. A spirit-warder and counter-summoner whose specialty is keeping things out, not bringing them in. Carries pouches of consecrated salt mixed with their own dried blood and has run out of the latter twice.

The Crow. A scout, runner, and message-carrier with a network of trained corvids. The birds are smarter than they have any right to be and increasingly will not deliver to certain districts no matter the price.

The Match. A pyromancer who works strictly at the small scale — fuses, signal-fires, controlled burns. Has not lit a fire willingly outside of work since the orphanage incident, which they will not discuss.

The Cord. A garrote-specialist and quiet-killer whose work is never linked back to the crew. Carries seven cords of different materials, each for a different sort of mark, and one is reserved for an occasion they won't name.

The Reel. A film-photographer and surveillance-runner with a hand-cranked camera and a dark-room of their own. Some of the negatives develop figures that weren't visible at the scene; they keep a private collection.

The Cog. A clockwork-tinker who can disable, reassemble, or repurpose any mechanical lock or trap in the city. Their workshop has a music box no one wound that occasionally plays a different tune than the one its drum should.
The Ash. A funerary-priest gone freelance who knows the rites for laying a ghost properly — and the rites for not laying it. Charges a sliding scale and the dead consider it fair.

The Pith. A torturer-turned-interrogator who got out of the business and into the trade, where the work is similar but the questions are sharper. Has rules now and breaks them less often than they used to.

The Rasp. A street-musician and signal-coder who passes information through tunes played on a cracked accordion. The accordion was their teacher's; sometimes it plays a tune they don't know in a key they've never learned.

The Hinge. A double-agent and informer who works two crews simultaneously and somehow has not been killed for it. The patron-spirit they answer to is older than either crew and finds the arrangement amusing.

The Drift. A canal-pilot and small-boat smuggler who knows the waterways better than the harbormaster. Has a deal with whatever lives under the third bridge and won't take a job that requires crossing it.
The Weft. A weaver of binding-cloth, the sort that holds spirits or testimony or contracts. Sells to ministry archivists and crime bosses alike and treats both with the same unflappable politeness.

The Talon. An animal-handler who runs hounds, hawks, and rats for the crew's purposes. The animals are devoted; the handler suspects they were devoted before being trained, which is a question they refuse to follow.

The Vellum. A revenant kept moving by the patron's contract — visibly dead but functional, useful for jobs no living person could survive. The crew pays the patron rather than the revenant. The revenant remembers what they used to be paid for.
The Gloam. A glamour-worker and illusionist who can cloak a person, an object, or a doorway for short stretches. The glamours leave a faint sweet smell that some hounds and some spirits can track.

The Tinder. A demolition-charge maker who specializes in alchemical and spirit-bound explosives. Each charge is wrapped in a personal sigil and the maker claims this is purely artistic; the charges that don't carry it occasionally fail.
The Yoke. A psychological pressure-specialist who breaks marks without leaving marks. Was trained by a Ministry interrogation school and walked out with the curriculum but not the badge.

The Plinth. A statuary-thief and architectural specialist who works strictly with stone — gargoyles, reliefs, the smaller sort of classical figure. Several of the pieces in their workshop appear to have changed pose between visits.

The Cinder. A cremation-specialist and evidence-disposer whose furnace has consumed corpses, contracts, and on one occasion an entire rival crew. Charges by the stone of weight and offers a discount for repeat clients.

The Mooring. A ship-binder and harbor-fixer who can dock, undock, or hide a vessel in a port without papers. Owes their position to an old agreement with the harbor's patron-spirit and is overdue on the offerings.
